_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Debug: Part 2
summary: Trace lifetime, signed bounds, mutation, and edge cases.
refresher: Harder C++ bugs may come from invalid references, unsigned arithmetic, shared mutation, or a missing boundary case. Record state near its first incorrect transition.

blocks.body: Harder C++ bugs may come from invalid references, unsigned arithmetic, shared mutation, or a missing boundary case. Record state near its first incorrect transition. Trace lifetime, signed bounds, mutation, and edge cases.

Deeper C++ debugging tracks object lifetime, signed and unsigned values, aliases, and mutation. A reference or pointer can outlive the object it once identified, becoming dangling. Container growth can also invalidate pointers, references, and iterators into its old storage.

blocks.caption: Using size-compatible indexes and a strict upper bound keeps each access valid.
Do not return a reference to a local object that is about to disappear.

blocks.body: The local text ends its lifetime when the function returns. The wrong reference then points at an object that no longer exists. Returning by value produces a valid result, and modern C++ can do this efficiently without exposing the local lifetime.

blocks.title: Inspect this state
blocks.body: Subtracting one from an empty container size wraps an unsigned value to a huge number; check emptiness before that calculation.

blocks.prompt: Why is `i <= v.size()` unsafe for indexing?
blocks.why: The count itself is one past the final valid index, yet the inclusive test allows it.
An empty size cannot be safely decremented as an unsigned value, so guard the case first.
